description: This figure presents electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) data for Fe–30Mn–8Al–1C–xCu low-density steels with varying copper content (0, 1, and 3 wt%) tested in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Subfigure (a) displays Nyquist plots showing the imaginary impedance (Zim) versus real impedance (Zre) in units of Ω/cm². The data reveal depressed semicircles characteristic of capacitive behavior, where the 0 Cu alloy (black squares) exhibits the largest arc radius corresponding to the highest charge transfer resistance (Rct ≈ 5796 Ω·cm²), while increasing Cu content progressively reduces the arc diameter, with the 3 Cu alloy (blue triangles) showing the smallest Rct (≈ 2062 Ω·cm²). An inset equivalent circuit model comprises solution resistance (Rs) in series with a parallel combination of Rct and constant phase element (CPE). Subfigure (b) presents Bode plots showing impedance modulus |Z| (left y-axis) and negative phase angle (right y-axis) versus frequency (Hz). The 0 Cu sample maintains the highest low-frequency impedance modulus (~6000 Ω/cm²) and broadest phase angle peak near 65°, indicating superior passive film stability. As Cu content increases, the low-frequency |Z| decreases and phase angle maxima shift to higher frequencies with reduced magnitude, signifying deteriorated corrosion resistance due to microgalvanic coupling effects between Cu-enriched grain boundaries and the austenitic matrix.
